results.possibleIdentity: Wade Zimmerman is an American photographer and designer who has been a prominent figure in architectural and design circles since the early 1970s. He is widely recognized as a premier architectural photographer, documenting the work of world-renowned architects such as Richard Meier, Christian de Portzamparc, David Adjaye, and Emilio Ambasz. Zimmerman's career reflects an early involvement in the New York art and architectural community; in 1973, he was the designer for the catalog of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) exhibition "Another Chance for Housing: Low-Rise Alternatives," which was co-sponsored by the Institute for Architecture and Urban Studies (IAUS). His photography is extensively published in major monographs (including Rizzoli's Richard Meier series) and architectural journals such as Architectural Record and Mark Magazine. Zimmerman has also documented significant art installations that intersect with technology and architecture, such as Fujiko Nakaya's fog sculptures, which were famously part of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70.

results.possibleIdentity: Barry Fess (Barry M. Fess, 1939–2012) was an artist and educator who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his role as the founding chairman of the Buffalo, New York chapter of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization established to foster collaboration between artists and engineers. Fess was an associate professor in the Department of Art at the State University of New York at Buffalo (SUNY Buffalo), where he taught sculpture and was a significant figure in the local art scene. His involvement with E.A.T. began around 1968 when he organized the Buffalo local group to facilitate matches between regional artists and technical experts. He was also associated with the Albright-Knox Art Gallery during the late 1960s, contributing to discussions and exhibitions on the intersection of art and technology.

results.possibleIdentity: Stafford Beer (1926–2002), formally known as Professor Dr. Stafford Beer, was a British cybernetician, management consultant, and artist (painter and poet) who was highly influential in the late 1960s and early 1970s. While primarily recognized for his pioneering work in cybernetics and the Viable System Model (VSM), he was deeply involved in the intersection of art and technology during the period when Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) was most active. Beer was a key figure in the landmark 1968 exhibition 'Cybernetic Serendipity' at the Institute of Contemporary Arts in London, where he exhibited his 'Stochastic Analog Machine' (SAM). His work often focused on complex systems, feedback, and homeostasis, themes that paralleled the goals of E.A.T. in promoting collaborations between artists and engineers. In the early 1970s, he led Project Cybersyn in Chile under the government of Salvador Allende, a project that is frequently cited in the context of media art and technological history alongside E.A.T. projects like '9 Evenings' and the Pepsi Pavilion. Following the military coup in Chile in 1973, Beer retreated to a cottage in Wales where he focused extensively on his artistic practice, producing a series of metaphysical paintings known as 'Requiem' and publishing several books of poetry.

results.possibleIdentity: Doloris Holmes is a performance artist, poet, and filmmaker who was active in the New York avant-garde and feminist art scenes starting in the early 1960s. She founded and directed the White Mask Theatre in New York City and was a prominent member of Women Artists in Revolution (W.A.R.), a radical feminist group that emerged from the Art Workers' Coalition in 1969. In 1970, she was among the artists who condemned the actions of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) and presented a list of demands to its director, John Hightower. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, she was involved in experimental projects such as an 'oxygen utility dome' (1969–1970) and conducted oral history interviews for the Archives of American Art with figures like Lee Krasner, Anaïs Nin, and Cliff Joseph. Following a 1972 trip to Spain, where she was inspired by prehistoric cave art, she divorced her husband, the poet Armand Schwerner, and created the performance 'Fish-Joy'. In May 1978, she adopted the name 'Janais'—a combination of Janis Joplin and Anaïs Nin—to symbolize her artistic rebirth. Her works, including the 1973 film 'Room of the White Mask' and the 1993 book 'Lady of the Grape Arbor', often explore themes of identity, femininity, and non-traditional performance.
